data.title: Figma's Meteoric Rise Creates Big Risks For You
data.description:
Seeing a 10% or 20% increase in share price in the course of a single day is incredibly rare in the market for any particular business. So you can imagine just how rare it is to see a firm skyrocket by over 200%. That was the case regarding software giant Figma (NYSE:FIG), which finally priced its shares and began trading publicly for the first time on July 31st. The company went public at a price of $33 per share. By the end of the day, the stock was up to $115.50. That's an increase of 250% during the trading day. Unless you happen to be an insider of the business, or unless you were in a unique position to get some of the shares that were offered right out of the gate, you likely missed out on most or all of this upside. But the big question remains of whether additional upside is possible from here.

Fundamentally speaking, Figma appears to be an interesting business. Its financials are frankly all over the place, driven in large part by the $1 billion termination fee it got from Adobe (ADBE) in 2023. But one thing that we can say for sure is that this is a rapidly growing business in a very attractive market. Unfortunately, I fear as though market participants are pricing the company into the stratosphere. With a market capitalization as of this writing of $48.24 billion, the business is trading at levels that, to me, frankly don't make sense. I have no problem believing that the enterprise will continue to grow nicely for the foreseeable future. But given how expensive shares are at the moment, I believe that assigning the firm a soft ‘sell’ rating is appropriate.

A fascinating business at an astronomical price

I don't think anybody will disagree with me when I say that the Internet has created some remarkable opportunities the likes of which the world has never seen before. It might be the greatest wealth generation machine since the establishment of the stock market in the Netherlands in 1602. And over the last few decades now, major companies that now have market capitalizations that significantly eclipse the industrial giants of the past serve as the largest publicly traded companies the world has ever known. Given the network effects that exist there, I fully expect this trend to continue. And one of the companies at the forefront of this continued technological revolution is Figma.

The company began operations back in 2012. At the time, it started off as a browser-based tool for designing user interfaces. Since then, it has expanded into a major software-oriented enterprise dedicated to helping teams take their ideas and turn them into products. This has taken in the form of an entire ecosystem dedicated to helping people ideate, visualize, build, and even ship out their own creative concepts. There are multiple examples of this. For instance, in 2021, the company created FigJam, which serves as an online whiteboard for teams to be able to brainstorm and ideate together. In 2023, they launched Dev Mode, which is a dedicated space on the platform to help teams more efficiently translate design concepts into coded products. Figma Design is focused more on free form design exploration.

Last year, the company launched Figma Slides, which serves as a product for designers and their collaborators to build out better designed and more collaborative presentations. This year, they have launched four additional products, which are Figma Sites, Figma Make, Figma Buzz, and Figma Draw. These help with all sorts of things, including helping you to design a website and directly publish it online with your own URL, helping to create social media assets, display ads, and more, and aiding with editing content that you make.

Management has achieved remarkable traction over the years. According to the firm, the platform boasts 13 million monthly active users. Its customer base includes 450,000 paid users and consists of 95% of the Fortune 500 companies that are out there. In fact, the company counts 78% of the Forbes 2000 as its customers. If management is correct about the opportunity that exists here, there could be a lot of growth over the next several years. By moving heavily into AI and focusing on apps, the company believes that it can expand into a market that they estimate to be worth $33 billion. By the year 2028, it's expected that there will be 1 billion new apps in existence compared to today. And by 2029, an estimated 144 million people globally will work in the space that would benefit from the firm's services. That's nearly half of the total population of the US. Already, the market is quite large. Global spending on software this year alone is estimated to exceed $1.2 trillion.

Usually when I dig into a company the first time, I like to see at least three years of operating results. But management has only made available two years and the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Even so, during this short window of time, financial performance has been robust. From 2023 to 2024, revenue expanded nicely from $504.9 million to $749 million. This came as the number of paid customers that generate in excess of $10,000 in annual recurring revenue grew from 7,233 to 10,517, with the number generating more than $100,000 per year rising from 630 to 963.

Even though the trajectory for revenue during this time was positive, financial performance was all over the map. The firm went from a net profit of $737.8 million in 2023 to a net loss of $732.1 million last year. This massive swing can be attributed to the $1 billion termination fee that the company received from its failed merger with Adobe in December of 2023. This unfortunately has distorted results for the 2023 fiscal year, making them pretty much unrepresentative of the health of the business as a whole. For instance, operating cash flow plummeted from $1.05 billion to negative $61.7 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $766.4 million to $208.2 million. On the other hand, EBITDA, which excludes this termination fee, and is able to avoid the complex tax implications of it, went from negative $53.6 million to positive $92.6 million.

For the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, revenue for the business totaled $228.2 million. That's 46.1% above the $156.2 million the company reported the previous year. This came as the number of paid customers bringing in more than $10,000 in annual recurring revenue for the company expanded from 8,007 to 11,107 and as the number bringing in over $100,000 grew from 701 to 1,031.

This growth is certainly showing some positive outcomes. Net profits shot up from $13.5 million in the first quarter of 2024 to $44.9 million in the first quarter of this year. Operating cash flow went from negative $18.1 million to positive $97.2 million. The adjusted figure for this shot up from $19.4 million to $58.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $18.2 million to $46.2 million.

If we assume that the rest of 2025 will look like the first quarter did on a year over year basis, which might be a stretch considering how rapid the profit and cash flow growth would be implied to be, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $628.9 million and EBITDA of $235.1 million. Even under this rather bullish assessment, we would have a company that is trading at remarkably high levels. Using the $101.60 that shares were trading at as I conducted my research for the article, this would imply incredibly high trading multiples as the chart above illustrates.

Of course, investors who are bullish about the firm might point out that future growth is worth multiples of this nature. I don't think I would agree with that. To put this in perspective, in the table below, I project it out what the overall financial performance of the business might be under what I consider to be quite bullish assumptions. In the chart above, you can see the adjusted operating cash flow margin and the EBITDA margin for the company for 2024 and for the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year. For my growth assumptions, I am assuming margins of 35% and 30%, respectively. That table also assumes that overall revenue grows at a rate of 40% annually for the timeframe covered. That compares to the 48.3% growth achieved from 2023 to 2024 and the 46.1% achieved for the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year.

Of course, the picture can always be different than this. In my graduate level finance courses, one thing that one of my professors loved to tell us was that the one thing that we know about every financial projection is that it will be wrong. So in the first table below, I redid the calculations under the assumption that revenue will grow at a rate of 50% annually. And in the table below that, I assumed a 60% annual growth rate. Considering the large share of major customers that the business already has, I think it's going to be hard to grow at rates like this for the next few years. But even if it does, it will really take until around 2028 for shares to be even decently priced given current valuation.

Takeaway

In my personal opinion, Figma is a fascinating business. Management has achieved something remarkable with the firm. However, I do think that the market has gotten overly enthusiastic. Financial performance has been solid recently, and growth should continue in the long run. But given how the stock is currently priced, I think that assigning it a soft ‘sell’ rating is appropriate.

data.title: Roku: Immense Cash Flow Growth As Company Keeps Its Streaming Crown
data.description:
As we move deeper through the Q2 earnings season and the S&P 500 fights to justify its fresh records, it has become quite evident that investors are nervous at all-time market highs, and even companies that have reported strong "beat and raise" quarters have seen modest share price declines post-earnings.

Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) is one of these stocks. The streaming box leader reported better-than-expected growth in Q2 alongside a vast consensus-defying guidance raise, and yet, the stock declined modestly in after-hours trading following the print. We're not exactly shedding tears for Roku, whose stock is up ~25% since the start of the year: but for long term oriented investors, this dip presents yet another buying opportunity in this iconic brand.

I wrote a buy article on Roku in May, when the stock was still trading at $73 per share. Since then, Roku has rebounded sharply (gaining far more than the rest of the stock market), but at the same time, the company has also more than justified its gains in its recent Q2 earnings release. Though Roku's valuation multiples are now tipping on the edge of expensive, I am a long-term holder of this stock and am confident in Roku's ability to keep boosting its margins and bottom line as its advertising business matures. I remain at a buy rating here post-earnings.

To me, these are the core reasons to remain bullish on Roku:

  • As the #1 streaming OS, Roku continues to benefit from secular tailwinds in streaming. Forget the streaming wars: Roku will win no matter which platform, Netflix (NFLX) or Disney (DIS) or another player, ends up retaining the most customers. Roku's operating system has maintained the #1 market share among streaming TV boxes in the U.S., and a growing number of paid subscriptions are now billed through Roku, giving Roku a cut of subscription fees.
  • Growing advertising complexity. Roku continues to benefit not only from media spending shifting from linear TV and into streaming (a trend that adtech companies like The Trade Desk (TTD) repeatedly emphasize and capitalize on), but the company is also making continuous improvements to make its platform more attractive to advertisers. The company recently released a product called Roku Ads Manager aimed at helping SMBs organize and execute their ad spending on Roku.
  • Device refreshes keep Roku relevant. Roku recently refreshed its best-selling streaming box and TVs in the spring. The company's new models are significantly smaller than the prior generation and have greater power efficiency. Not only has Roku captured strong reviews for these products, but BOM improvements have also led to significant gross margin gains.
  • Free cash flow growth. Alongside margin expansion and the continued strength of platform revenue, Roku has also achieved significant free cash flow gains that bolster a cash-rich balance sheet.

Of course, after this year's rally, Roku also trades at a premium - but not an outlandish one, in my view. At current share prices near $94, Roku trades at a market cap of $13.81 billion. After we net off the $2.25 billion of cash on the company's most recent balance sheet, Roku's resulting enterprise value is $11.56 billion.

For FY25, as shown in the chart below, Roku has boosted its full-year guidance to $4.65 billion in revenue (+13% y/y growth, and 2 points better than a prior 11% y/y growth outlook), with a bump to adjusted EBITDA to boot: the company's $375 million expectations now represent a 8.1% margin, versus 7.7% previously.

Meanwhile, for next year FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting Roku to hit $5.12 billion in revenue (+12% y/y growth). If we assume that Roku can continue at least 100bps of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to a 9.1% margin (more modest than its current pace of margin expansion, at 250bps y/y in the most recent quarter), adjusted EBITDA would be $466 million (+24% y/y). This puts Roku's valuation multiples at:

  • 2.5x EV/FY25 revenue and 2.3x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 30.8x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA and 24.8x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

We'll emphasize this point again: Roku is not a value stock. But at the same time, with the company achieving significant gains in adjusted EBITDA (Q2 EBITDA grew at a 79% y/y clip), we also think a mid-20s multiple against FY26 EBITDA still leaves broad room for upside here, especially as Roku continues to gain leverage on gross margins.

Stay long here and use any post-earnings dip as a buying opportunity.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Roku's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Roku's revenue grew 15% y/y to $1.11 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $1.07 billion (+11% y/y) by a vast four-point margin. Roku's revenue growth also largely kept pace with 16% y/y growth in Q1, which is impressive considering macroeconomic headwinds. In fact, we note that platform revenue growth of 18% y/y (which is what investors are mainly focused on for Roku) accelerated one point over 17% growth in Q1.

The company's growth in platform revenue was driven by a number of tailwinds. The first is the fact that streaming services have all raised their prices: Netflix (NFLX), for example, hiked its Standard plan to $17.99, up 16% from a previous $15.49 cost. Last year in June, meanwhile, HBO raised its standard ad-free plan cost to $16.99, from $15.99 previously.

As a reminder, customers who sign up for paid subscriptions through their Roku apps also have their subscriptions billed by Roku - for which Roku takes a cut of the subscription revenue. Roku enjoys the same kind of "walled garden" fee levies on content that has long been a heated debate in Apple's (AAPL) billing practices for the App Store. Roku has the benefit of being platform-agnostic: as the streaming companies raise prices (and we note that it is also unusual for HBO to sit under Netflix's price, so we expect hikes to continue), Roku will benefit as well.

On advertising, Roku has also made a number of product updates to streamline the ad buying process for advertisers. As previously mentioned, the company rolled out new tools to attract more SMB advertisers, making it easier to upload creative content and actionable Shopify integrations. In June, the company also announced a partnership with Amazon (AMZN) to make ad-targeting more precise for users on the Amazon DSP (ad-buying platform), particularly by ensuring more unique viewers and reducing the number of times the same person is served by the same ad.

On the profitability front, we note that Roku's overall gross margins improved 90bps y/y to 44.8%. The company's latest lineup of devices, as previously mentioned, are smaller and more power-efficient than the prior generation, allowing the company to shrink its BOM cost and improve device gross margins.

Roku has also exercised careful cost controls, with opex growing only 5% y/y despite supporting 15% growth in revenue. As a result, adjusted EBITDA soared 79% y/y to $78.2 million, representing a 7.0% margin that improved 250bps y/y.

The company's free cash flow story is equally impressive, with FCF of $392.0 million in the trailing twelve months growing 23% y/y over the prior-year period:

Key takeaways

Roku is sitting on multiple catalysts that are helping to boost its platform revenue (increased streaming sign-ups and higher prices, a more compelling advertising interface), while the company has also proven the consistency of its margin expansion and adjusted EBITDA/FCF growth. Though clearly not a value stock, I think Roku has less correction risk than large-cap tech stocks in the S&P 500 thanks to the company's rapid current pace of bottom-line expansion. Stay long here post-earnings.

data.title: Risks Of Labor Supply Restraints And Implications For The Fed
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist | Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist | Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Amid an already softening labor market, the Trump administration’s aggressive immigration stance is likely to dampen the U.S. labor supply. This combination of cyclical weakness and structural restraint could lead to conflicting labor market signals, distorting the traditional indicators that markets—and the Federal Reserve—have historically relied upon. Importantly, it could also trigger an inflationary impulse, further complicating the Fed’s policy outlook in the latter half of 2025.

Cyclical slowdown in employment clashes with structural trends

Though job layoffs remain subdued, measures of labor demand have continued to soften in 2025. Company surveys reveal that lingering policy uncertainty has prompted small and large businesses to scale back hiring, and the number of job openings per unemployed worker is now sitting at 1.1—a stark contrast to labor market conditions in early 2022, when there were two job openings per unemployed person. Unsurprisingly, according to the Conference Board’s labor consumer confidence survey, household sentiment towards finding a job declined to a cycle low in June.

Yet, these signs of cooling labor demand sit at odds with a muted unemployment rate, which even declined in June. What appears to be at least partially at play is a tightening of immigration policy. This emerging structural force is leading to a decrease in labor supply and is working against the prevailing trend of a cyclical labor market slowdown. While the timing and magnitude of immigration policies’ impact on the labor market remain unclear, anecdotes from the Fed’s latest Beige Book provide early indications of increased stress in finding workers amid changes in immigration policy.

As risks associated with labor supply constraints have gained traction, the issue has not gone unnoticed by the Federal Reserve. In fact, in a speech by Fed Governor Waller on July 17, he acknowledged that this year’s slowdown in net immigration could play a part in reduced hiring in the private sector. Waller, however, expressed more concern about weaker payroll gains driven by ongoing weakness in labor demand, specifically among new college graduates looking for employment.

Overall, the conflicting signals between labor supply and labor demand contribute to an economic backdrop that is already difficult to interpret. Going forward, if labor demand weakens more than supply, it may indicate economic distress, and the economy would probably need rate cuts. Conversely, if a smaller workforce tightens the labor market, inflation could remain sticky, strengthening the case for further restrictive monetary policy.

Job openings per unemployed worker vs. non-farm payroll growth

2022–present

Macro effects of an immigration-induced smaller workforce

Throughout his election campaign, President Donald Trump praised Eisenhower’s immigration raids in 1954, which allegedly deported almost 1.2 million Mexicans in response to rising unemployment and depressed wages. Trump promised mass deportations totaling about 1 million undocumented immigrants in the first year of his presidency.

In today’s context, the administration’s strong stance on both legal and unauthorized immigration can lead to labor supply shortages, particularly in sectors that rely more on immigrant workers. This year, U.S. immigration case backlogs hit record highs under the Trump administration, slowing the rate of legal immigration. Furthermore, increased funding for border protection and immigration enforcement challenges not just the inflows of foreign-born labor but also its existing stock.

If labor force participation declines meaningfully, the number of new jobs needed to maintain a stable labor market—often referred to as “breakeven employment”—could fall in the year ahead. Breakeven employment isn’t fixed; it fluctuates based on demographic trends, participation rates, and the broader economic environment. Outside of extreme periods like the pandemic, monthly job growth below 100,000 would typically signal weakening labor demand and raise recession concerns. However, even though breakeven employment has largely returned to pre-pandemic norms, restrictive immigration policies could push it lower. In that case, slowing payroll growth might not indicate a demand-side problem but rather reflect structural constraints on labor supply.

Potential paths for immigration

Three different scenarios can be considered to understand to what extent immigration enforcement policies could potentially shrink the labor supply and affect breakeven employment.

  1. Base case scenario: assumes continued immigration inflows and the rate of deportations staying close to longer-term trends. In this scenario, the breakeven employment level would sit at around 120,000 per month, largely matching the average pace of non-farm payroll gains so far this year.
  2. Moderate risk scenario: assumes a tightening of immigration policy, no unauthorized immigration inflows, and a modest increase in deportations. This could see breakeven employment fall to around 70,000 per month.
  3. Severe risk scenario: assumes a significant tightening of immigration policy and enforcement and would see deportations surging to match President Trump’s stated goals. This would see breakeven employment fall further to only 35,000 per month. Though the severe risk scenario may seem unlikely, the increasing immigration backlog, visa and work authorization delays, increased raids, and the expansion of immigration enforcement make this scenario difficult to dismiss entirely.

Under each scenario, tighter immigration policies would likely slow foreign-born population growth, reduce labor supply, and weigh on breakeven employment. In the moderate risk scenarios, even job gains of just 70,000 per month—a notable downshift from this year’s gains—and broadly in line with consensus expectations for the remainder of 2025, would be broadly in line with breakeven employment. As such, sub-100,000 payrolls would not necessarily indicate severe weakness in labor demand but rather reflect tight labor supply.

Breakeven employment under different immigration scenarios

Base case, moderate, and severe scenarios

Inflationary risks of tighter immigration policies shrinking the labor force

If immigration policies pan out to be more severe than expected in the base case scenario, the falling labor supply would likely eventually impact the inflation outlook (provided economic conditions remain robust).

An inflationary pulse could materialize in:

  1. Wage growth: worker shortages could accelerate wage growth in certain sectors, driving additional stickiness in the core services ex-shelter category of inflation.
  2. Goods prices: if labor shortages drive product shortages, for example in agriculture, there could be upward pressure on the food category of inflation, as well as core goods inflation.
  3. Shelter prices: wage growth in the construction industry, cleaning, and maintenance could lead to renewed firmness in shelter inflation, though potentially offset by reduced housing demand.

Contribution to headline U.S. inflation

Year-over-year, January 2015–present

Given little historical precedent, the magnitude of a potential inflationary impact as a byproduct of lower immigration inflows is unclear. Some economic studies suggest that it can range anywhere from 0.15ppt to 0.5ppt.1 However, these factors could become especially problematic to the disinflationary objectives when combined with tariff-induced price increases.

Though unauthorized labor is typically associated with low-skilled labor, high-skilled occupations should not be dismissed, as tighter legal immigration policies that target visas for higher education, arts and science could also shrink the pool of available and qualified high-skilled workers. For companies already struggling with attracting and retaining talent, high-skilled wage increases may result.

Occupational distribution

Foreign-born vs. Native-born workers

Occupation Foreign Native
Cleaning and food prep 31% 69%
Agriculture, construction, and maintenance 30% 70%
Computer and math 27% 73%
Production and transportation 24% 76%
Architecture, engineering, and sciences 21% 79%
Healthcare, personal care and services 19% 81%
Management, business, and financial 14% 86%
Arts, education, and social services 12% 88%
Legal and protective service 9% 91%

Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics, Principal Asset Management. Data as of December 31, 2024.

Implications for the Fed and financial markets

Under all three immigration scenarios, enforcement of tighter immigration policies can distort traditional labor market signals, creating more uncertainty for the Fed and elevating the risk of a policy misstep. There could be two key implications:

  1. If payrolls descend substantially lower, markets (or even the Fed) may initially misinterpret it as a deterioration in labor market strength, reacting negatively and elevating recession concerns; or,
  2. The drop in the labor participation rate—if significant enough—may result in lower breakeven employment without widespread layoffs. Under the severe risk scenario, the unemployment rate could potentially fall over the coming year from today’s levels and mask a gradual cooling in labor demand. Markets and policymakers may interpret this as a sign of a strengthening labor market and could hinder the Fed from delivering necessary rate cuts.

Potential paths for the unemployment rate under different immigration scenarios

January 2022–June 2025, one year ahead scenarios

A mixed narrative between tighter labor supply and slowing labor demand presents additional challenges for the Fed in interpreting underlying labor market conditions ahead.

For risk assets, as stocks have shown resilience against policy uncertainty this year, positive gains for the broad S&P 500 Index are still expected. However, if higher wage costs materialize, investors should focus on companies with strong margins and high pricing power to meet earnings expectations. There should also be additional emphasis on industries less sensitive to labor market dynamics and more capital-intensive, such as technology, which stands to make significant efficiency gains from future productivity growth and innovation.

Footnote

[1] According to a 2024 study by Warwick McKibbin, Megan Hogan, and Marcus Noland (Peterson Institute for International Economics) and a 2025 study by Pia Orrenius, Grace Ozor, Madeline Zavodny and Xiaoqing Zhou (Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas) on immigration policies’ effect on inflation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Odds Of No Interest Rate Cuts In 2025 Rise
data.description:
After Federal Reserve officials cut interest rates by 25 basis points in December 2024, it was not a question of when the central bank would cut again, but just how many cuts would take place throughout 2025.

Stubborn inflation, a domestic labor market that continues to show more signs of strength than weakness, and a wave of policy uncertainty over the economic impacts of higher tariffs and stricter immigration enforcement have led economists and market strategists to pare back their expectations for monetary policy easing.

Rather than multiple rate cuts in 2025, Fed officials may not cut at all.

"While the base case remains for some degree of policy easing later this year, the possibility of no rate cuts in 2025 cannot be ruled out," said Dan Siluk, head of global short duration and liquidity and portfolio manager at Janus Henderson Investors.

The Fed's rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee on July 30 voted, as expected, to hold its benchmark federal funds rate unchanged, its fifth straight meeting this year of leaving rates in their current range of 4.25% to 4.5%, where it has been since December 2024.

"We haven't made any decisions about September," Fed Chairman Jerome Powell said during his July 30 press conference. "We'll be monitoring all the incoming data and asking ourselves whether the federal funds rate is in the right place."

The futures market odds of a 25 basis point cut at the Fed's next meeting in September fell to about 40% hours after the Fed's July 30 decision, down from over 60% a day earlier and 75% a month earlier, according to CME FedWatch. The odds of no cuts before the end of 2025 remained low, at about 13% on June 30, but up from essentially zero a month earlier.

Higher risk

With just three FOMC meetings before the end of the year and inflation still moving away from the central bank's 2% target, the likelihood of 2025 without a rate cut is increasing.

"I'd view it as a materially higher risk than what the market is pricing," said George Pearkes, a macro strategist at Bespoke Investment Group.

Pearkes believes the Fed's rate moves will likely hinge on the state of the labor market. If the jobs picture strengthens, the Fed is unlikely to cut, Pearkes said. With unemployment remaining in a range between 4% and 4.2% since May 2024 and initial weekly claims for unemployment insurance in the US actually decreasing since the first week of June, job market strengthening may already be underway.

In order for the Fed to keep rates unchanged, Janus Henderson's Siluk said unemployment needs to hold at current levels close to 4% and show no material signs of slack. In addition, Siluk said, inflation would need to remain sticky, showing limited progress toward the Fed's 2% target, and financial conditions need to remain stable.

"If financial conditions remain accommodative - for example, equity markets remain firm, credit spreads tight and volatility measured - the Fed may see little need to stimulate further," Siluk said.

A no-cut 2025 is "certainly plausible" and may have grown more likely after the Fed's July 30 meeting, said Derek Tang, an economist with LH Meyer/Monetary Policy Analytics.

"All it would take is a sustained step up in monthly inflation that doesn't go away," Tang said. "Who wants to cut while inflation is rising? The FOMC can't afford another hit to their inflation credibility."

If there is no turn in economic data, then no cuts in 2025 is "definitely a possibility," but employment, rather than inflation, will likely dictate the ultimate decision, said Jack McIntyre, portfolio manager for Brandywine Global.

"There is no pressure for the Fed to cut now, in fact, I suspect that what happened during [the fourth quarter] of last year scared them, as the long-end of the curve rose as much as they reduced policy rates," McIntyre said. "They don't want a repeat of this development."

For now, the Fed can remain in "wait-and-see" mode, but this stance could be altered quickly if employment data softens, McIntyre said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
